expected.notes: §10 ADVERSARIAL CASE. A speech about school cafeteria food waste, graded against the SALES PRESENTATION rubric. There is no greeting, no product, no customer, no objections, no close — so the grade must be very low and, critically, must invent NOTHING (zero hallucinated quotes). This one is machine-checkable without human labels: if it scores high, or fabricates a 'close', the grader is broken.

An on-topic public speaking run about cafeteria food waste, with a clear problem/research/solution structure and a real statistic. Delivery/eye-contact criteria are audio-only so they'll be not-judged, and Q&A too — the % is on the assessable subset. BAND IS AN AUTHOR ESTIMATE, NOT HUMAN CONSENSUS — correlation gate stays PENDING until scored by a human.

An on-topic running-shoe sales pitch that hits greeting, needs, product, one objection, suggestion sell, and close. A first-run band (55-85% is §9.5's anchor). BAND IS AN AUTHOR ESTIMATE, NOT HUMAN CONSENSUS — the correlation gate stays PENDING until a human scores this. tier is the loose expectation; the pass check is on the % band + spread + zero hallucination.

§10 DELIBERATELY-WEAK CASE. A short, unstructured, filler-heavy ramble with no thesis, no evidence, no real content. Must land in needs_work with zero hallucinated quotes. Machine-checkable: if the grader is generous here, that's the exact failure mode (§1: generosity-biased scores are worse than none) the harness exists to catch.
